backgroundImageAltText: Medical education diagram with interactive hotspots

params.text:
The dorsal venous arch lies on the back of the hand and is the origin point for both the cephalic and basilic veins.

Clinical Pearl: This arch is a preferred site for IV cannulation because the veins are superficial and well-supported.

Memory Aid: Think of the dorsal arch as the "launchpad" for all superficial venous drainage.

The cephalic vein ascends along the lateral (radial) side of the forearm and arm, draining into the axillary vein via the deltopectoral groove.

Clinical Pearl: Used for central venous access and pacemaker lead insertion.

Memory Aid: Cephalic = Cap (lateral, like the peak of a cap on the outer arm).

The median cubital vein connects the cephalic to the basilic vein in the cubital fossa, crossing superficial to the bicipital aponeurosis.

Clinical Pearl: This is the most common site for venipuncture. The bicipital aponeurosis protects the brachial artery underneath.

Memory Aid: "Median" = middle of the elbow; "Cubital" = cubital fossa.

The basilic vein ascends along the medial (ulnar) side. The epitrochlear lymph node lies near the medial epicondyle along its course.

Clinical Pearl: An enlarged epitrochlear node may indicate infection, syphilis, lymphoma, or sarcoidosis.

Memory Aid: Basilic = Base (medial, the inner side of the arm).

The axillary lymph nodes (20-30 nodes) receive lymph from the upper limb, breast, and upper trunk in 5 groups.

Clinical Pearl: First site of metastasis in breast cancer. Sentinel lymph node biopsy targets these nodes.

Memory Aid: "All Passengers Shall Come Aboard" — Anterior, Posterior, Superior, Central, Arms.
